svg.aria-label: The permission boundary around code execution and computer-use tools. In the center is a box labeled SANDBOX holding the two dangerous tool classes, code execution and computer-use, and built from four controls. The first control is an action allowlist, including an AST allowlist of safe node types and a few safe calls for code, plus a command allowlist of read_file and write_file for computer-use. The second is a resource budget reused from Part 8. The third is no-network isolation, so external hosts are denied. The fourth is idempotent side effects reused from Part 9, so a retried write does not double-act. On the left, legitimate operations pass through the boundary: compute round sum orders over len orders giving 173.33, read_file report.txt, and write_file out.txt with an idempotent retry. On the right, dangerous operations bounce off the boundary: rm report.txt is blocked as a disallowed function call, exfiltration to evil.com is blocked because http_get is not on the allowlist and the network is closed, and reading secrets.txt is blocked as a disallowed function call. A prominent footnote states this is illustrative; a real boundary is OS-level using gVisor or Firecracker.
svg.title: The sandbox permission boundary: allowlist, budget, no-network, and idempotency let legit ops through and bounce dangerous ops off

text.font-family: Inter, ui-sans-serif, system-ui, -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, sans-serif

svg.text: In-process Python sandboxing (AST allowlists, stripped builtins) is unsound; determined code escapes it. The damage on the unsafe path is simulated; no untrusted code is really run.

text.font-family: Inter, ui-sans-serif, system-ui, sans-serif

svg.text: Part 16 turns this into a full agentic-security pipeline.
